docs.text: The official reference documentation for Rux.

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Enums define a closed set of named variants for a single type.

An enum is useful when a value can be one of several shapes, but the shapes are still part of the same conceptual type.

In Rux, enums can be:

  • unit variants with no payload
  • tuple-like variants with ordered payload fields
  • named-field variants with explicit field names
  • optionally assigned an explicit integer discriminant
  • optionally marked pub

Enums are one of the most important types in the language because they bridge the gap between simple tag values and structured variant data.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
enum Status {
    Ok,
    NotFound = 404,
    Failed = 500,
}

enum Message: uint8 {
    Ping,
    Data(char8[]),
    Named {
        kind: uint32;
        value: uint64;
    },
}

An enum declaration may include:

  • an optional pub modifier
  • a name
  • an optional integer base type
  • a comma-separated list of variants
  • tuple-like payloads in parentheses
  • named-field payloads in braces
  • an optional discriminant on each variant

docs.title: What an Enum Means
docs.text:
An enum says that a value belongs to exactly one of a fixed set of alternatives.

That is different from a struct, where all fields exist together. It is also different from a union, where fields share storage but the language does not automatically name the active variant for you.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Use an enum when the alternatives have clear names and the active case matters.

enum Result {
    Ok,
    Err,
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not use an enum just to avoid writing a normal structured type.

// bad idea: this is trying to smuggle a record into a variant list
enum UserLike {
    Person {
        name: char8[];
        age: uint32;
    },
    Company {
        name: char8[];
        employees: uint32;
    },
}

That may be valid, but it only makes sense when the program truly has mutually exclusive shapes.

docs.title: Enum Declaration Syntax
docs.text:
The general shape of an enum is:

enum Name [: BaseType] {
    Variant,
    Variant = 123,
    Variant(Type, Type),
    Variant {
        field: Type;
    },
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
enum Color {
    Red,
    Green,
    Blue,
}

docs.title: Public enums
docs.text:
A type may be marked pub.

pub enum TokenKind {
    Ident,
    Number,
    String,
}

Public visibility controls whether the enum is visible outside the defining scope, using the same visibility rules as the rest of the language.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The parser recognizes enum declarations at top level.
  • Variants are separated by commas.
  • A trailing comma before } is rejected.
  • Enum fields inside payload variants use their own syntax rules.

docs.title: Base Types
docs.text:
An enum may optionally specify an integer base type after a colon.

enum Code: uint8 {
    A = 1,
    B = 2,
}

The base type is the type of the enum discriminant tag.

docs.title: Default base type
docs.text:
If no base type is written, the compiler uses int.

enum Plain {
    A,
    B,
}

On the current target model, int is 8 bytes.

docs.title: Allowed base types
docs.text:
The semantic analyzer requires the base type to be an integer type.

That means things like uint8, uint16, uint32, uint64, int8, int16, int32, int64, int, and uint are valid base types, while floats and pointers are not.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Choose the base type deliberately when the enum must match a file format, ABI, or network protocol.

enum PacketType: uint8 {
    Ping = 1,
    Pong = 2,
    Data = 3,
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not rely on the default base type when the numeric size matters outside the compiler.

// bad idea if this crosses an ABI boundary
enum WireCode {
    A,
    B,
    C,
}

That version is readable, but the exact storage width is not as explicit.

docs.title: Variants
docs.text: Each variant names one possible case of the enum.

docs.title: Unit variants
docs.text:
A unit variant has no payload.

enum State {
    Idle,
    Busy,
    Stopped,
}

Unit variants are the cleanest form of enum variant.

docs.title: Tuple-like variants
docs.text:
A variant may hold ordered payload fields.

enum Response {
    Ok(uint32),
    Err(char8[]),
}

The fields are positional, so the order matters.

docs.title: Named-field variants
docs.text:
A variant may hold named fields.

enum Message {
    Move {
        x: int32;
        y: int32;
    },
    Color {
        r: uint8;
        g: uint8;
        b: uint8;
    },
}

Named-field variants behave like structured payloads.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Use tuple-style payloads when the meaning is naturally positional.

enum Vec2State {
    Point(float32, float32),
}

Use named fields when the payload carries semantically distinct values.

enum Vec2State {
    Point {
        x: float32;
        y: float32;
    },
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not use named fields when the names add no information.

enum Bad {
    Pair {
        a: int32;
        b: int32;
    },
}

That is not wrong, but it often reads like noise unless a and b actually mean something.

docs.title: Discriminants
docs.text:
A variant may be given an explicit integer discriminant.

enum HttpStatus: uint16 {
    Ok = 200,
    NotFound = 404,
    InternalError = 500,
}

The discriminant is the numeric value associated with that variant.

docs.title: Automatic numbering
docs.text:
If a variant does not specify a discriminant, the compiler assigns one automatically.

The first unit is 0, and each later unit increments from the previous value unless overridden.

enum Mode {
    Off,      // 0
    On,       // 1
    Sleep = 8,
    Deep,     // 9
}

docs.title: Literal forms
docs.text:
The parser and semantic layer accept integer literal text in a few common forms, including:

  • decimal
  • hexadecimal
  • binary
  • octal
  • negative values

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Use explicit discriminants for stable public APIs, binary formats, and protocol definitions.

enum Opcode: uint8 {
    Read = 1,
    Write = 2,
    Close = 3,
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not depend on auto-incremented values if the numbers are part of an external contract.

// bad idea for wire formats
enum Opcode {
    Read,
    Write,
    Close,
}

That looks fine in source code, but the numeric meanings can become a maintenance trap if the declaration changes later.

docs.title: Variant list separators
docs.text:
Variants are separated by commas.

enum Status {
    Ok,
    Err,
}

A trailing comma before the closing brace is rejected.

docs.title: Payload and discriminant cannot mix
docs.text: A variant cannot have both payload fields and an explicit discriminant when the parser and semantic checks expect them to remain separate in that form.

docs.title: Named payload validation
docs.text:
Named-field variants validate their own field list.

Duplicate field names are rejected, and every field type must resolve correctly.

docs.title: Layout and Size
docs.text:
Enums are sized using the base type plus the largest payload layout among all variants that carry data.

That means the compiler does not treat every enum as a magic black box. It computes a real layout model.

docs.title: Base storage
docs.text:
The tag width comes from the enum base type.

Examples:

  • uint8 → 1 byte
  • uint16 → 2 bytes
  • uint32 → 4 bytes
  • uint64 → 8 bytes
  • int → 8 bytes on the current target model

docs.title: Payload storage
docs.text:
For payload variants, the compiler computes the payload layout using the same kind of field-layout rules used for structured data:

  • fields are placed in order
  • each field is aligned by its size, capped at 8 bytes
  • padding may be inserted between fields
  • the resulting payload layout is rounded up to the payload alignment

docs.title: Size formula
docs.text:
The current enum size model works like this:

  1. compute the tag size from the base type
  2. compute each payload variant size and alignment
  3. take the largest payload size and alignment
  4. place the payload after the tag with alignment padding if needed
  5. round the whole enum up to the final alignment

In shorthand:

size = align_up(
    align_up(tagSize, payloadAlign) + payloadSize,
    max(tagAlign, payloadAlign)
)

If the enum has no payload variants, the total size is just the tag size.

docs.title: Example: unit enum
docs.text:
enum State: uint8 {
    Idle,
    Busy,
    Stopped,
}

Typical size:

  • tag = 1 byte
  • payload = none
  • total = 1 byte

docs.title: Example: small payload enum
docs.text:
enum MaybeInt: uint8 {
    None,
    Some(int32),
}

Typical size:

  • tag = 1 byte
  • payload = 4 bytes
  • payload alignment = 4 bytes
  • total = 8 bytes after padding

That extra padding is not wasted in the compiler’s model; it is there so the payload stays aligned.

docs.title: Example: named-field payload enum
docs.text:
enum Command: uint16 {
    Move {
        x: int32;
        y: int32;
    },
}

Typical layout reasoning:

  • tag = 2 bytes
  • payload fields are laid out like a struct
  • padding may be inserted after the tag and between payload fields
  • total size is aligned to the final enum alignment

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Keep payload variants reasonably small when the enum is used heavily.

enum TokenValue: uint8 {
    KindA,
    KindB(uint32),
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not assume the enum is always a tiny tag just because the syntax looks compact.

enum BigThing {
    Small,
    Huge(SomeLargeStruct),
}

A payload variant can make the overall enum much larger than a plain tag.

docs.title: Bits, Bytes, and Discriminants
docs.text:
The discriminant is the numeric identity of the current variant.

The compiler stores and reasons about discriminants as integer values, and the base type determines their width.

docs.title: Bit-width examples
docs.text:
• uint8 tag → 8 bits
  • uint16 tag → 16 bits
  • uint32 tag → 32 bits
  • uint64 or int tag → 64 bits on the current target model

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Bit-width affects:

  • size on disk or in memory
  • ABI compatibility
  • how much padding is needed
  • whether a variant value fits a chosen base type

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Use the smallest tag width that can safely represent all variants.

enum SmallCode: uint8 {
    A = 1,
    B = 2,
    C = 3,
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not use a huge tag type if the values are tiny and stable.

enum SmallCode: uint64 {
    A = 1,
    B = 2,
    C = 3,
}

That works, but it is bloated for no benefit.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text: Enum construction depends on the variant shape.

docs.title: Unit variants
docs.text:
Unit variants behave like simple values.

let s = State::Idle;

docs.title: Tuple-like variants
docs.text:
Tuple-like variants are constructed with positional arguments.

let r = Response::Ok(123);

docs.title: Named-field variants
docs.text:
Named-field variants are constructed with brace syntax.

let m = Message::Move {
    x: 10,
    y: 20,
};

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Unit variants do not need payload syntax.
  • Payload variants must supply the payload shape expected by the variant.
  • Named-field variants must provide the required named fields.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Match the construction form to the variant form.

enum Event {
    Tick,
    Move { x: int32; y: int32; },
    Data(uint32),
}

let a = Event::Tick;
let b = Event::Move { x: 1, y: 2 };
let c = Event::Data(42);

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not mix construction styles randomly.

let bad = Event::Move(1, 2);

If the variant is named-field based, the brace form is the one that communicates intent best.

docs.title: Pattern Matching
docs.text:
Enums are intended to work naturally with match.

match value {
    State::Idle => 0,
    State::Busy => 1,
    _ => 2,
}

Payload variants can bind payload data in patterns.

match value {
    Response::Ok(code) => Print(code),
    Response::Err(msg) => Print(msg),
    _ => Print("unknown"),
}

Named-field variants can bind fields by name.

match value {
    Message::Move { x, y } => Print(x + y),
    _ => Print(0),
}

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Prefer exhaustive matches when the set of variants is small and stable.

match state {
    State::Idle => ...,
    State::Busy => ...,
    State::Stopped => ...,
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not rely on a default arm to hide missing cases if the enum is central to the logic.

match state {
    _ => ...,
}

That is legal in many contexts, but it can make the code less explicit than it should be.

docs.title: Simple enum
docs.text:
enum Color {
    Red,
    Green,
    Blue,
}

docs.title: Enum with explicit base type
docs.text:
enum Opcode: uint8 {
    Read = 1,
    Write = 2,
    Close = 3,
}

docs.title: Enum with tuple payloads
docs.text:
enum Response {
    Ok(uint32),
    Err(char8[]),
}

docs.title: Enum with named-field payloads
docs.text:
enum Command {
    Move {
        x: int32;
        y: int32;
    },
    Stop,
}

docs.title: Public enum
docs.text:
pub enum TokenKind {
    Ident,
    Number,
    String,
}

docs.title: Missing variant name
docs.text:
enum Bad {
    ,
}

docs.title: Duplicate variant names
docs.text:
enum Bad {
    A,
    A,
}

docs.title: Invalid base type
docs.text:
enum Bad: float32 {
    A,
}

docs.title: Duplicate named field in a variant
docs.text:
enum Bad {
    Move {
        x: int32;
        x: int32;
    },
}

docs.title: Mixing payload and discriminant incorrectly
docs.text:
enum Bad {
    Item(123) = 1,
}

docs.title: Missing field in a named variant construction
docs.text:
enum Command {
    Move {
        x: int32;
        y: int32;
    },
}

let c = Command::Move { x: 1 };

docs.title: Unknown field in a named variant construction
docs.text: let c = Command::Move { x: 1, z: 2 };

docs.title: Enum values are tagged values
docs.text: An enum value is identified by its tag, and payload variants carry extra data alongside that tag.

docs.title: The size model is real
docs.text:
The compiler computes enum size using tag size, payload size, and alignment.

That matters for stack allocation, ABI decisions, and lowering.

docs.title: Explicit discriminants are the stable option
docs.text: If a number must never silently change, write the number yourself.

docs.title: Keep payloads intentional
docs.text: Payload variants are powerful, but they also make the enum larger and more complex.

docs.title: No generic enums in the reviewed parser
docs.text: The reviewed parser shows enum Name ..., not a generic enum Name<T> ... form.

docs.title: Implementation Notes
docs.text: This section describes how the compiler currently handles enums internally.

docs.title: Parsing
docs.text:
The parser recognizes:

  • enum Name { ... }
  • optional : BaseType
  • unit variants
  • tuple-like variants
  • named-field variants
  • optional = discriminant
  • comma-separated variants

docs.title: Semantic analysis
docs.text:
The semantic analyzer checks:

  • the base type is an integer type
  • variant names are unique
  • named-field names are unique inside each variant
  • payload fields resolve correctly
  • a variant does not mix incompatible forms in the checked representation

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions are callable declarations that group executable behavior behind a name.

A function may be:

  • a normal function with a body
  • a signature-only declaration
  • an operator function
  • a method declared inside an extend block
  • an extern declaration bound to an external symbol
  • an asm function backed by hand-written assembly

Functions may also be generic, variadic, overloaded, and optionally public.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

func PrintLine(text: char8[]);

func Add(a: float64, b: float64) -> float64 {
    return a + b;
}

func +(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> Vector {
    return Vector { x: a.x + b.x, y: a.y + b.y, z: a.z + b.z };
}

A function declaration may include:

  • an optional pub modifier
  • an optional asm prefix
  • a name
  • optional type parameters
  • a parameter list
  • an optional return type
  • either a body or a trailing semicolon

docs.title: Regular functions
docs.text:
Regular functions are declared with func.

func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

These are the standard callable declarations used for ordinary code.

docs.title: Signature-only functions
docs.text:
A function may be declared without a body.

func PrintLine(text: char8[]);

This form is useful for declarations that are defined elsewhere or only needed as a signature.

docs.title: Operator functions
docs.text:
Functions may use operator names.

func +(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> Vector {
    return Vector { x: a.x + b.x, y: a.y + b.y, z: a.z + b.z };
}

Operator functions participate in overload resolution like normal named functions.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
Methods are functions declared inside an extend block.

extend Vector {
    func Length(self) -> float64 {
        return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y + self.z * self.z);
    }
}

Methods are called using the . syntax on a value, and the compiler resolves the correct method based on the receiver type.

docs.title: External functions
docs.text:
External functions are declared with extern.

extern func CreateFileA(name: char8[], access: uint32) -> *opaque;

These declarations describe symbols that are provided by a DLL, system library, or another external object file.

docs.title: Assembly functions
docs.text:
A function may be marked asm.

asm func FastAdd(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32;

Assembly functions are part of the function system, but their implementation is expected to come from assembly rather than normal Rux source.

docs.title: Function Declaration Syntax
docs.text:
The general shape of a function is:

func Name<TypeParams>(params) -> ReturnType {
    body
}

or:

func Name(params) -> ReturnType;

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
pub func Clamp(value: int32, min: int32, max: int32) -> int32 {
    if value < min {
        return min;
    }
    if value > max {
        return max;
    }
    return value;
}

docs.title: Public functions
docs.text:
pub marks the function as publicly visible.

pub func Exported() -> int32 {
    return 1;
}

Public visibility is especially important for packages and libraries.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
A function parameter has a name and a type.

func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

docs.title: Parameter syntax
docs.text:
name: Type

Example:

func Square(value: int32) -> int32 {
    return value * value;
}

docs.title: Parameter names
docs.text:
Parameter names are part of the declaration and are available inside the function body.

func Mix(left: int32, right: int32) -> int32 {
    return left + right;
}

docs.title: Parameter types
docs.text:
Every non-variadic parameter has a type.

func PrintNumber(value: int32);

The compiler uses parameter types for type checking, overload resolution, and lowering.

docs.title: Return Types
docs.text:
A function may declare a return type with ->.

func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

If no return type is written, the compiler treats the function as returning an opaque or implicit default return type depending on the compilation stage.

func Log(text: char8[]) {
    Print(text);
}

docs.title: Why explicit return types matter
docs.text:
Explicit return types help with:

  • readability
  • overload resolution
  • API stability
  • FFI compatibility
  • compiler diagnostics

docs.title: Type Parameters
docs.text:
Functions may be generic.

func Identity<T>(value: T) -> T {
    return value;
}

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: func Name<T, U>(params) -> ReturnType

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
func First<T>(value: T) -> T {
    return value;
}

Type parameters are part of the function signature and influence type resolution.

docs.title: Variadic Functions
docs.text:
A function may accept a variadic parameter.

func PrintAll(...items: char8[]) {
    // ...
}

Or in parameter form:

func Sum(values: int32...) -> int32 {
    // ...
}

Variadic parameters collect extra arguments into a slice-like value during lowering.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
func LogAll(prefix: char8[], values: int32...) {
    // ...
}

docs.title: Call behavior
docs.text:
Variadic calls can be made in two ways:

  • by passing separate trailing arguments
  • by passing a spread argument when the language supports it

Example:

LogAll("sum", 1, 2, 3);

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Variadic parameters are treated specially by the compiler.
  • Only the last parameter may be variadic.
  • Extra arguments beyond the fixed parameters are gathered into a slice.

docs.title: Default Parameters
docs.text:
A parameter may have a default value.

func DrawLine(x: int32, y: int32 = 0) {
    // ...
}

This allows a caller to omit trailing arguments when the compiler can safely fill them in.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
func CreatePoint(x: float64, y: float64 = 0.0) -> Point {
    return Point { x: x, y: y };
}

docs.title: Call usage
docs.text:
CreatePoint(5.0);
CreatePoint(5.0, 7.0);

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Default arguments are used for omitted trailing parameters.
  • Defaults are applied in order from left to right.
  • Default values must be compatible with the parameter type.

docs.title: Common mistake
docs.text:
func Foo(a: int32 = 1, b: int32) {
    // ...
}

Defaults only help when the omitted arguments are at the end of the call.

docs.title: Calling Functions
docs.text:
Functions are called by writing the function name followed by parentheses.

Add(1, 2);

docs.title: Argument checking
docs.text:
The compiler checks:

  • number of arguments
  • argument types
  • variadic usage
  • spread usage
  • whether overload resolution can find a match

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

let result = Add(10, 20);

docs.title: Result type
docs.text: The result of a call expression is the function’s return type.

docs.title: Overloads
docs.text:
Rux allows multiple functions with the same name.

func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

func Add(a: float64, b: float64) -> float64 {
    return a + b;
}

docs.title: Overload selection
docs.text:
The compiler chooses a candidate based on the argument types.

In practice, overload resolution prefers:

  1. exact matches
  2. assignable matches
  3. compatible variadic forms when relevant

If the compiler cannot prove a better match, it falls back to a reasonable candidate rather than leaving the call unresolved.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Add(1, 2);       // picks the int overload
Add(1.0, 2.0);   // picks the float overload

docs.title: Overload names in lowering
docs.text:
If a function name is overloaded, the compiler mangles the callee name using the parameter types so each overload gets a distinct internal symbol.

That means the source-level name stays the same, but the backend sees a unique implementation name.

docs.title: Methods and extend
docs.text:
Methods are functions attached to a type through extend.

struct Vector {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
    z: float64;
}

extend Vector {
    func Length(self) -> float64 {
        return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y + self.z * self.z);
    }
}

docs.title: Instance methods
docs.text:
Instance methods receive self as their first parameter.

func Length(self) -> float64 {
    return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y + self.z * self.z);
}

When called, the compiler automatically supplies the receiver as the first argument.

let len = value.Length();

docs.title: Static methods
docs.text:
Methods that do not use self behave like associated functions.

extend Vector {
    func New(x: float64, y: float64, z: float64) -> Vector {
        return Vector { x: x, y: y, z: z };
    }
}

These are called with :::

let v = Vector::New(1.0, 2.0, 3.0);

docs.title: Method resolution
docs.text:
The compiler looks up methods based on the receiver type.

If the receiver is a pointer, that pointer type is used directly. If the receiver is a value, the compiler takes its address when needed so methods can still receive self as a pointer.

docs.title: Method overloads
docs.text:
Methods can also be overloaded.

extend Vector {
    func Scale(self, factor: float64) -> Vector {
        return Vector { x: self.x * factor, y: self.y * factor, z: self.z * factor };
    }

    func Scale(self, factor: int32) -> Vector {
        return self.Scale(float64(factor));
    }
}

docs.title: Operator Methods
docs.text:
Functions may implement operators for a type.

extend Vector {
    func +(self, other: Vector) -> Vector {
        return Vector { x: self.x + other.x, y: self.y + other.y, z: self.z + other.z };
    }
}

This lets values of the type participate in operator syntax.

let sum = a + b;

Operator methods are still functions, so they follow the same type and overload rules as regular methods.

docs.title: External Functions
docs.text:
External functions describe symbols that live outside Rux source.

extern func MessageBoxA(text: char8[], caption: char8[]) -> int32;

docs.title: External blocks
docs.text:
Rux also supports grouped external declarations:

extern {
    func CreateFileA(...);
    func WriteFile(...);
    handle: *opaque;
}

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text:
External functions may be decorated with attributes such as:

  • @[Import(lib: "...")]
  • @[Call(.Win64)]
  • @[Target("Windows")]

These control import libraries, calling conventions, and target-specific availability.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@[Import(lib: "Kernel32.dll")]
extern func GetCurrentProcessId() -> uint32;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• External declarations usually do not have bodies.
  • They are intended for FFI and platform integration.
  • The compiler uses the declaration to generate the correct link and call information.

docs.title: Calling Conventions
docs.text:
Functions may use a calling convention.

@[Call(.Win64)]
extern func GetTickCount64() -> uint64;

Calling conventions matter when interfacing with foreign code, especially on Windows.

They affect how arguments are passed and how the backend emits calls.

docs.title: Empty Bodies and Signatures
docs.text:
A function can be declared as a pure signature.

func DoSomething(value: int32);

This is useful when only the type information is needed.

A normal function with a body is written with braces:

func DoSomething(value: int32) {
    Print(value);
}

docs.title: Simple function
docs.text:
func Add(a: int32, b: int32) -> int32 {
    return a + b;
}

docs.title: Function with default parameter
docs.text:
func Greet(name: char8[] = "world") {
    Print("Hello, ");
    Print(name);
}

docs.title: Generic function
docs.text:
func Identity<T>(value: T) -> T {
    return value;
}

docs.title: Variadic function
docs.text:
func Sum(values: int32...) -> int32 {
    var total = 0;
    for value in values {
        total += value;
    }
    return total;
}

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
extend Point {
    func Length(self) -> float64 {
        return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y);
    }
}

docs.title: Operator function
docs.text:
extend Point {
    func +(self, other: Point) -> Point {
        return Point { x: self.x + other.x, y: self.y + other.y };
    }
}

docs.title: External function
docs.text:
@[Import(lib: "Kernel32.dll")]
extern func GetCurrentProcessId() -> uint32;

docs.title: Missing parameter type
docs.text:
func Bad(x) {
}

docs.title: Wrong number of arguments
docs.text: Add(1);

docs.title: Calling an immutable-typed or unresolved overload incorrectly
docs.text: Add("hello", "world");

docs.title: Invalid reassignment inside the function body
docs.text:
func Test() {
    let x = 1;
    x = 2;
}

docs.title: Invalid variadic usage
docs.text:
func Log(values: int32...) {
}

Log(1, 2, ...);

docs.title: Missing body when a body is required
docs.text:
func Compute(x: int32) -> int32

Depending on context, the compiler expects either a body or a trailing semicolon.

docs.title: Functions are first-class at the type level
docs.text:
The compiler tracks function signatures as types.

That is why functions can be resolved, overloaded, lowered, and passed through the backend in a structured way.

docs.title: Methods are still functions
docs.text: A method is not a separate language species. It is a function attached to a type, with self handled specially by the compiler.

docs.title: Overload names may be mangled internally
docs.text: Source code uses the same name for overloaded functions. The backend uses a mangled form so each overload can be emitted as a distinct symbol.

docs.title: Variadic arguments become a slice-like value
docs.text: Trailing variadic arguments are collected into a slice representation during lowering.

docs.title: Default arguments are inserted during lowering
docs.text: If a call omits trailing parameters with defaults, the compiler fills them in before reaching the lower-level representation.

docs.title: Implementation Notes
docs.text: This section describes how the compiler currently handles functions internally.

docs.title: Parsing
docs.text:
The parser recognizes:

  • normal function declarations
  • generic parameters
  • parameter lists
  • optional return types
  • bodies or signatures
  • asm func
  • extern func
  • methods inside extend
  • operator function names

Parameters can also be marked variadic or given default values.

docs.title: Semantic analysis
docs.text:
The semantic analyzer records function symbols, resolves signatures, checks argument compatibility, and handles overload sets.

Function overloads are grouped under the same name.

When a function is called, the analyzer selects a matching overload using argument count and assignability rules.

docs.title: Method resolution
docs.text:
Method lookup is based on the receiver type.

The analyzer also handles interface methods and associated function types so that method calls and method references can be typed correctly.

docs.title: Pointers
docs.text:
Pointers represent memory addresses rather than values.

A pointer stores the address of another object and allows indirect access to that object's storage.

Pointers are a fundamental part of Rux and are commonly used for:

  • low-level memory manipulation
  • FFI interoperability
  • operating system APIs
  • dynamic data structures
  • slice and interface implementations

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
let value = 42;

let ptr: *int32 = &value;

let current = *ptr;

*ptr = 100;

let raw: *opaque = ptr;

let empty: *int32 = null;

Pointer-related syntax:

*T
*const T

&value
*pointer

null

value as *T

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Pointers refer to memory locations rather than directly storing values.

A pointer type always refers to another type called the pointee type.

*int32

The above type represents a pointer to an int32.

*Point

The above type represents a pointer to a Point.

Pointers are strongly typed.

A pointer to one type is not automatically interchangeable with a pointer to another type.

docs.title: Mutable Pointers
docs.text:
Mutable pointers are declared using:

*T

Example:

var value = 10;

let ptr: *int32 = &value;

*ptr = 20;

The pointer may be dereferenced to read or modify the referenced value.

docs.title: Const Pointers
docs.text:
The parser recognizes:

*const T

Example:

let text: *const char8;

This syntax is commonly used when interacting with foreign APIs that expect read-only memory.

Example:

extern {
    func Print(
        text: *const char8
    );
}

docs.title: Opaque Pointers
docs.text:
Untyped pointers use:

*opaque

Example:

let handle: *opaque;

opaque represents an intentionally uninterpreted type.

It is most commonly used for:

  • operating system handles
  • foreign library objects
  • generic FFI pointers
  • raw memory references

Example:

extern {
    func CreateHandle() -> *opaque;
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Any pointer type may be assigned to *opaque.

let ptr: *int32 = &value;

let raw: *opaque = ptr;

This behavior is implemented directly in the compiler's assignability rules.

docs.title: Address-Of Operator
docs.text:
The unary & operator creates a pointer.

let value = 5;

let ptr = &value;

The resulting type is a pointer to the operand's type.

Example:

let number: int32 = 42;

let ptr = &number;

Result:

*int32

docs.title: Dereferencing
docs.text:
The unary * operator dereferences a pointer.

let value = *ptr;

Dereferencing accesses the value stored at the memory location referenced by the pointer.

Example:

let number = 42;

let ptr = &number;

let copy = *ptr;

Result:

copy == 42

docs.title: Writing Through a Pointer
docs.text:
Dereferencing may also be used on the left side of an assignment.

*ptr = 100;

Example:

var number = 42;

let ptr = &number;

*ptr = 100;

Result:

number == 100

docs.title: Null Pointers
docs.text:
Rux provides a dedicated null pointer literal.

null

Example:

let ptr: *int32 = null;

Null represents the absence of a valid memory address.

Common uses:

  • optional references
  • uninitialized handles
  • FFI APIs
  • sentinel values

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The parser recognizes null as a built-in keyword.

During lowering, null is represented internally as a zero-valued pointer.

docs.title: Casting Pointers
docs.text:
Pointers may participate in explicit casts.

value as Type

Example:

let raw: *opaque;

let ptr = raw as *int32;

Pointer reinterpretation should always be explicit.

The compiler does not automatically convert between unrelated pointer types.

docs.title: Type Compatibility
docs.text: Pointer compatibility is intentionally strict.

docs.title: Valid
docs.text:
let ptr: *int32 = &value;

let raw: *opaque = ptr;

docs.title: Invalid
docs.text:
let ptr: *float64 = &value;

where:

value: int32

The pointee types do not match.

docs.title: Pointer Size
docs.text:
All pointer types occupy a fixed size.

Current size:

Type Size *T 8 bytes *const T 8 bytes *opaque 8 bytes

This size is used by the compiler's internal layout model.

docs.title: Pointers and Slices
docs.text:
Slices internally contain a pointer and a length.

Example:

let values: int32[];

Conceptually:

struct Slice<T> {
    data: *T;
    length: uint;
}

A slice therefore occupies:

16 bytes

on current targets.

docs.title: Pointers and Interfaces
docs.text:
Interface values internally contain pointer data.

Conceptually:

struct Interface {
    data: *opaque;
    vtable: *opaque;
}

The compiler treats interface values as a pair of pointers.

docs.title: FFI Usage
docs.text:
Pointers are heavily used for interoperability.

Example:

@[Import(lib: "Kernel32.dll")]
extern {
    func CloseHandle(
        handle: *opaque
    ) -> bool32;
}

Example:

let handle = CreateHandle();

CloseHandle(handle);

docs.title: Basic Pointer
docs.text:
let value = 42;

let ptr = &value;

Print(*ptr);

docs.title: Modifying a Value
docs.text:
var count = 10;

let ptr = &count;

*ptr = 20;

docs.title: Opaque Pointer
docs.text: let handle: *opaque;

docs.title: Null Pointer
docs.text: let ptr: *int32 = null;

docs.title: Cast
docs.text:
let raw: *opaque;

let typed = raw as *int32;

docs.title: Dereferencing a Non-Pointer
docs.text:
let value = 10;

let x = *value;

Error:

cannot dereference non-pointer type

docs.title: Invalid Assignment
docs.text:
let ptr: *int32 = 10;

Error:

cannot assign integer to pointer

docs.title: Incompatible Pointer Types
docs.text:
let ptr: *float64 = &value;

where:

value: int32

Error:

incompatible pointer types

docs.title: Parsing
docs.text:
The parser recognizes:

*T

*const T

for pointer types.

The parser also recognizes:

&expr

and

*expr

as unary operators.

docs.title: Type System
docs.text:
Pointers are represented internally using:

TypeRef::Kind::Pointer

The pointee type is stored as the pointer's inner type.

Example:

*int32

becomes:

Pointer(
    Int32
)

docs.title: Assignability
docs.text:
The semantic analyzer contains a special compatibility rule:

*T -> *opaque

This conversion is implicit.

Other pointer conversions require explicit casts.

docs.title: Lowering
docs.text:
Pointers are lowered as native machine-address values.

Current pointer size:

8 bytes

The backend treats pointer values as address-sized storage locations.

docs.title: Structs
docs.text:
Structs define user-owned composite types made from named fields.

A struct groups related values together into a single record-like type with a fixed field order and a compiler-defined memory layout.

Structs are one of the core data types in Rux and are used for everything from small value objects to low-level runtime structures.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
struct Point {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
}

let p = Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 };

let x = p.x;

Struct-related syntax includes:

  • struct declarations
  • public fields
  • generic struct parameters
  • struct construction
  • field access
  • struct patterns
  • methods via extend

docs.title: Struct Declarations
docs.text:
A struct is declared with the struct keyword.

struct Point {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
}

Each field has:

  • a name
  • a type
  • an optional pub modifier

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
struct Person {
    pub name: char8[];
    age: uint32;
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Fields are written in declaration order.
  • Each field ends with a semicolon.
  • Struct declarations may appear at module level or inside nested modules.

docs.title: Generic Structs
docs.text:
Structs may declare type parameters.

struct Box<T> {
    value: T;
}

Type parameters are parsed, preserved through semantic analysis, and carried into lowering.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
struct Pair<T, U> {
    first: T;
    second: U;
}

docs.title: Construction with type arguments
docs.text: let p = Pair<int32, float64> { first: 1, second: 2.5 };

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Generic type arguments appear in angle brackets.
  • The compiler preserves generic type information in the type system and lowering layers.
  • Backend code generation for generics is a separate concern from parsing and semantic tracking.
  • Generics are not yet implemented in the backend.

docs.title: Field Rules
docs.text:
A struct field is declared as:

name: Type;

Example:

struct Size {
    width: uint32;
    height: uint32;
}

docs.title: Public fields
docs.text:
A field may be marked public:

struct Token {
    pub kind: uint32;
    value: char8[];
}

Public fields are intended to be visible outside the defining module or package according to the language’s visibility rules.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Field names must be identifiers.
  • Field types may be any valid Rux type.
  • Fields cannot currently be declared without a type.

docs.title: Struct Construction
docs.text:
A struct value can be constructed using brace syntax.

let p = Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 };

docs.title: Explicit construction
docs.text:
let person = Person {
    name: "Alice",
    age: 30
};

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Field names are written explicitly in the initializer.
  • The initializer order does not need to match the declaration order unless the compiler or front end enforces an order rule.
  • The parser supports generic struct initialization syntax.

docs.title: Example with generics
docs.text: let box = Box<int32> { value: 42 };

docs.title: Field Access
docs.text:
Fields are accessed using the . operator.

let p = Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 };
let x = p.x;

Example:

Print(person.name);

Field access is a normal postfix expression and participates in later semantic checks and lowering.

docs.title: Struct Patterns
docs.text:
Structs can be destructured in pattern position.

let Point { x, y } = p;

Or with explicit field patterns:

let Point { x: px, y: py } = p;

Struct patterns are used in bindings and match arms.

docs.title: Example in a match
docs.text:
match value {
    Point { x: 0, y } => Print(y),
    _ => Print(0)
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Struct patterns must match the struct type shape.
  • Field patterns are parsed separately from struct expressions.
  • Pattern syntax is part of the parser’s supported feature set, not an afterthought.

docs.title: Methods and extend
docs.text:
Methods can be attached to structs with extend.

struct Vector {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
    z: float64;
}

extend Vector {
    func Length(self) -> float64 {
        return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y + self.z * self.z);
    }
}

docs.title: Instance methods
docs.text:
Instance methods take self as their first parameter.

func Length(self) -> float64 {
    return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y + self.z * self.z);
}

docs.title: Static methods
docs.text:
A method without self behaves like an associated function.

extend Vector {
    func New(x: float64, y: float64, z: float64) -> Vector {
        return Vector { x: x, y: y, z: z };
    }
}

Called as:

let v = Vector::New(1.0, 2.0, 3.0);

docs.title: Operator methods
docs.text:
A struct may define operator methods inside extend.

extend Vector {
    func +(self, other: Vector) -> Vector {
        return Vector {
            x: self.x + other.x,
            y: self.y + other.y,
            z: self.z + other.z
        };
    }
}

These are resolved by the compiler like ordinary methods, but using operator syntax.

docs.title: Interface Implementations
docs.text:
The parser supports extend in interface-related forms as well.

extend Vector : Display {
    func ToString(self) -> String {
        return "Vector";
    }
}

or:

extend Display for Vector {
    func ToString(self) -> String {
        return "Vector";
    }
}

This allows a struct to implement an interface by attaching the required methods.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The compiler tracks methods by type.
  • Interface implementation is associated with the concrete type name.
  • Interface method tables are used later during lowering and backend generation.

docs.title: Visibility
docs.text:
Structs may be public.

pub struct Point {
    pub x: float64;
    pub y: float64;
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Public visibility can apply to the struct itself.
  • Public visibility can also apply to individual fields.
  • Visibility is tracked by the parser and semantic analyzer.

docs.title: Memory Layout
docs.text:
Struct layout is determined by the compiler.

The current layout model preserves field order and computes offsets using alignment rules.

docs.title: General rules
docs.text:
• Fields are laid out in declaration order.
  • Each field is aligned based on its size.
  • The overall struct size is padded up to the maximum alignment.
  • Alignment is capped at 8 bytes in the current layout model.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
struct Point {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
}

A typical layout for this struct is:

Field Offset Size x 0 8 y 8 8

Total size:

16 bytes

docs.title: Another example
docs.text:
struct Mixed {
    a: uint8;
    b: uint32;
}

A typical layout is:

Field Offset Size a 0 1 b 4 4

The compiler inserts padding between fields when required to satisfy alignment.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Field offsets are compiler-defined, not arbitrary.
  • Field order is preserved.
  • Layout depends on the field types and their sizes.
  • Named fields that themselves resolve to structured types may contribute their own layout and alignment.

docs.title: Size Rules
docs.text: The compiler’s size model is used by semantic analysis, lowering, and backend code generation.

docs.title: Primitive field sizes
docs.text:
Common field sizes are:

  • bool8, char8, int8, uint8 → 1 byte
  • bool16, char16, int16, uint16 → 2 bytes
  • bool32, char32, int32, uint32, float32 → 4 bytes
  • int64, uint64, int, uint, float64, pointers, String, function values → 8 bytes
  • slices → 16 bytes
  • opaque → 0 bytes

docs.title: Struct size computation
docs.text:
For each field:

  1. determine the field’s size
  2. compute alignment as the field size clamped to 8 bytes
  3. align the current offset
  4. place the field
  5. advance the offset by the field size

After all fields are placed, the total size is aligned to the maximum field alignment.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
struct Small {
    a: uint8;
    b: uint8;
    c: uint8;
}

Total size is typically 3 bytes, then rounded up to the struct alignment boundary.

docs.title: Nested structs
docs.text:
Nested structs contribute their own size and alignment.

struct Outer {
    inner: Point;
    tag: uint8;
}

The compiler uses the known layout of Point when computing the layout of Outer.

docs.title: Nested Structs and Named Types
docs.text:
Struct fields may themselves be named types.

struct Address {
    city: char8[];
    zip: uint32;
}

struct Person {
    name: char8[];
    address: Address;
}

The compiler resolves the named field type during semantic analysis and uses the resolved type during layout computation.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Named fields are not flattened automatically.
  • The struct contains the nested type as a field.
  • Layout is computed recursively through known named types.

docs.title: Simple struct
docs.text:
struct Point {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
}

docs.title: Struct with visibility
docs.text:
pub struct Config {
    pub enabled: bool;
    pub port: uint32;
}

docs.title: Generic struct
docs.text:
struct Box<T> {
    value: T;
}

docs.title: Nested struct
docs.text:
struct Rectangle {
    topLeft: Point;
    bottomRight: Point;
}

docs.title: Struct construction
docs.text: let p = Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 };

docs.title: Struct pattern
docs.text: let Point { x, y } = p;

docs.title: Method implementation
docs.text:
extend Point {
    func Length(self) -> float64 {
        return Sqrt(self.x * self.x + self.y * self.y);
    }
}

docs.title: Missing field type
docs.text:
struct Bad {
    x;
}

docs.title: Missing semicolon after a field
docs.text:
struct Bad {
    x: int32
}

docs.title: Invalid construction field
docs.text: let p = Point { a: 1.0, y: 2.0 };

docs.title: Duplicate field names
docs.text:
struct Bad {
    x: int32;
    x: int32;
}

docs.title: Pattern mismatch
docs.text:
let Point { x, y, z } = p;

when Point does not have a z field.

docs.title: Parsing
docs.text:
The parser recognizes struct declarations with:

  • struct Name { ... }
  • optional generic type parameters
  • field declarations
  • optional public fields

It also recognizes struct initialization and struct patterns in expression and pattern position.

docs.title: Semantic analysis
docs.text:
The semantic analyzer stores struct declarations, tracks their methods, and records whether a type implements an interface.

It also preserves type parameters and uses named struct types during type resolution.

docs.title: Lowering
docs.text:
Struct values are lowered to concrete layouts with field offsets and total size.

The backend computes:

  • field offsets
  • field sizes
  • total struct size
  • alignment

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
Types describe the shape, size, and compatibility of values in Rux.

Rux has a small set of built-in primitive types and a set of compound type forms built from them.

The compiler tracks types through parsing, semantic analysis, HIR, and lowering, so the same type concept usually appears in more than one stage of compilation.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
let a: int32 = 42;
let b: float64 = 3.14;
let c: bool = true;
let d: *int32 = null;
let e: int32[] = [1, 2, 3];
let f: (int32, float64) = (1, 2.5);

Rux supports:

  • primitive scalar types
  • pointer types
  • slice types
  • tuple types
  • range types
  • function types
  • named types
  • type parameters
  • type aliases

docs.title: Built-in Primitive Types
docs.text: Rux includes several built-in primitive types.

docs.title: Boolean types
docs.text:
bool8
bool16
bool32
bool

bool is an alias for bool8.

The compiler treats all boolean widths as mutually assignable across the boolean family.

docs.title: Character types
docs.text:
char8
char16
char32
char

char is an alias for char32.

Character literals can also be prefixed to select a character width.

docs.title: Signed integer types
docs.text:
int8
int16
int32
int64
int

int is a platform-width integer type.

On 64-bit targets, it behaves like a 64-bit signed integer.

docs.title: Unsigned integer types
docs.text:
uint8
uint16
uint32
uint64
uint

uint is a platform-width unsigned integer type.

On 64-bit targets, it behaves like a 64-bit unsigned integer.

docs.title: Floating-point types
docs.text:
float32
float64
float

float is an alias for float64.

docs.title: Opaque type
docs.text:
opaque

opaque represents an untyped, zero-sized, or intentionally uninterpreted value category in the compiler’s type system.

It is commonly used for raw FFI handles and pointer-like interoperability.

docs.title: Type Aliases
docs.text:
Rux supports type aliases.

type Bytes = uint8[];
type Count = uint;

A type alias gives another name to an existing type.

Aliases are resolved during semantic analysis, but they remain meaningful in documentation and code structure.

docs.title: Named Types
docs.text:
A named type is a user-defined type name such as a struct, enum, union, interface, or alias target.

struct Point {
    x: float64;
    y: float64;
}

let p: Point = Point { x: 1.0, y: 2.0 };

Named types are the normal way to refer to user-defined data structures.

They also appear in generic instantiations such as:

Box<int32>

docs.title: Pointer Types
docs.text:
Pointer types use the * prefix.

*int32
*opaque
*const int32

Pointers reference memory locations rather than values directly.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
let ptr: *int32 = null;
let raw: *opaque = ptr;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• *const T is a const-qualified pointer form.
  • *opaque is the most permissive pointer-like form in the type system.
  • Any pointer is implicitly assignable to *opaque.

This is the closest equivalent to a void* style pointer in C.

docs.title: Slice Types
docs.text:
Slices use the [] suffix.

int32[]
char8[]
Point[]

Slices represent a sequence of elements.

They are used heavily for arrays, string-like data, and variable-length collections.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let values: int32[] = [1, 2, 3];

docs.title: Runtime shape
docs.text:
Slices are represented as a pair of values:

  • data pointer
  • length

That is why slice-like values occupy 16 bytes in the lowering pipeline.

docs.title: Tuple Types
docs.text:
Tuples use parentheses.

(int32, float64)
(bool, char8, uint32)

Tuples group values of possibly different types into one composite value.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let pair: (int32, int32) = (10, 20);

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Tuple elements are ordered.
  • Tuple field access may use numeric field names like 0, 1, 2 in compiler internals.
  • Tuple size is the sum of element sizes in the current size model.

docs.title: Range Types
docs.text:
Ranges model a lower bound, upper bound, and inclusive/exclusive behavior.

The compiler recognizes range syntax in type positions and carries range types through the type system.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let r: Range<int32> = 0..10;
let r2: Range<int32> = 0...10;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Range types are parameterized by an element type.
  • In lowering, ranges are treated as structured values with lo, hi, and inclusive fields.
  • Range size depends on the element type and alignment rules.

docs.title: Function Types
docs.text:
Functions have a first-class type representation in the compiler.

A function type looks like:

func(param1, param2, ...) -> returnType

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let f: func(int32, int32) -> int32;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Function types preserve parameter order.
  • The final element in the internal representation is the return type.
  • Variadic parameters are handled specially and are not stored as normal parameters in the internal function type list.

docs.title: Type Parameters
docs.text:
Rux supports type parameters in the compiler type system.

func Identity<T>(value: T) -> T {
    return value;
}

struct Box<T> {
    value: T;
}

Type parameters are represented in the compiler as unresolved type variables.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Type parameters are preserved through parsing, semantic analysis, and lowering.
  • They are not the same as concrete named types.
  • They occupy no fixed byte size until specialized into a concrete type.

docs.title: Pointer syntax
docs.text:
*T
*const T

docs.title: Tuple syntax
docs.text: (T, U)

docs.title: Slice syntax
docs.text:
T[]
T[N]

docs.title: Named type syntax
docs.text:
Name
Namespace::Name
Name<T>

docs.title: Self type
docs.text:
self appears in method and implementation contexts as the current receiver type.

func Length(self) -> float64

docs.title: Type Inference
docs.text:
The compiler infers types in many places when the surrounding context makes the type clear.

let x = 42;
let y = 3.14;
let z = true;

Inferred types are determined from the initializer and any surrounding expected type.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Inference is convenient for local code.
  • Explicit annotations are better when precision or clarity matters.
  • If the compiler cannot infer a type, it will report an error or keep the type unknown for recovery.

docs.title: Type Compatibility
docs.text: Rux does not treat all numeric types as freely interchangeable.

docs.title: Exact matching
docs.text:
Most numeric types must match exactly unless an explicit cast is used.

let x: int32 = 5;
let y: int64 = x; // not always allowed implicitly

docs.title: Implicit widening
docs.text:
Some widening conversions are accepted automatically.

  • float32 can widen to float64
  • int8, int16, int32 can widen to int
  • uint8, uint16, uint32 can widen to uint
  • int64 and int interoperate
  • uint64 and uint interoperate

docs.title: Boolean compatibility
docs.text: Boolean types are mutually assignable across boolean widths.

docs.title: Pointer compatibility
docs.text: Any pointer is implicitly assignable to *opaque.

docs.title: Unknown types
docs.text: Unknown types are treated leniently during recovery and intermediate compilation stages.

docs.title: Size and Layout
docs.text: The compiler tracks type size for code generation and storage allocation.

docs.title: Fixed-size primitives
docs.text:
Typical sizes in bytes:

  • bool8, char8, int8, uint8 → 1
  • bool16, char16, int16, uint16 → 2
  • bool32, char32, int32, uint32, float32 → 4
  • int64, uint64, int, uint, float64, pointers, String, function values → 8
  • slices → 16

docs.title: Compound sizes
docs.text:
• tuples are the sum of their element sizes in the current model
  • ranges depend on the element size and alignment
  • named types resolve to their underlying structure where possible

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Unknown and type-parameter sizes are not fixed until more type information is available.
  • opaque has size 0 in the compiler’s size model.
  • Some backend paths treat named types with generic arguments as concrete runtime-sized values.

docs.title: Strings
docs.text: Rux distinguishes between string-like values and the compiler’s internal string type representation.

docs.title: String type
docs.text: The type system includes a String-like built-in representation.

docs.title: String literals
docs.text:
String literals often lower into slice-like values with a character element type.

"hello"
c8"hello"
c16"hello"
c32"hello"

The element type depends on the literal prefix.

docs.title: Character Literals
docs.text:
Character literals may be prefixed to choose width.

'a'
c8'a'
c16'a'
c32'a'

The type of a character literal depends on the prefix.

docs.title: Numeric Literals
docs.text: Numeric literals may include base prefixes and type suffixes.

docs.title: Base prefixes
docs.text:
42
0x2A
0b101010
0o52

docs.title: Suffixes
docs.text:
1i8
1i16
1i32
1i64
1i
1u8
1u16
1u32
1u64
1u
1f32
1f64

Suffixes help lock the literal to a specific type.

docs.title: Unsuffixed integers
docs.text:
Unsuffixed integer literals can be checked against the target type.

If the value does not fit, the compiler emits an out-of-range diagnostic.

docs.title: Primitive values
docs.text:
let a: int32 = 42;
let b: float64 = 12.5;
let c: bool = true;

docs.title: Pointer and slice values
docs.text:
let p: *int32 = null;
let s: uint8[] = [1, 2, 3];

docs.title: Tuple value
docs.text: let pair: (int32, int32) = (1, 2);

docs.title: Generic type usage
docs.text:
struct Box<T> {
    value: T;
}

let x: Box<int32> = Box<int32> { value: 5 };

docs.title: Range value
docs.text: let r: Range<int32> = 0..10;

docs.title: Type mismatch
docs.text: let x: int32 = 3.14;

docs.title: Invalid pointer assignment
docs.text: let p: *int32 = 10;

docs.title: Out-of-range integer literal
docs.text: let x: uint8 = 1000;

docs.title: Using an unknown type parameter as a concrete size
docs.text:
func Foo<T>(value: T) {
    let size = sizeof<T>;
}

If the compiler cannot determine a concrete size, it will keep the type unresolved until more information is available.

docs.title: Implementation Notes
docs.text: This section reflects what the compiler currently does internally.

docs.title: Type representation
docs.text:
The compiler uses an internal TypeRef structure with kinds such as:

  • Unknown
  • Opaque
  • booleans
  • characters
  • signed and unsigned integers
  • floating-point numbers
  • pointers
  • slices
  • ranges
  • tuples
  • named types
  • type parameters
  • function types

docs.title: Type printing
docs.text:
Types have a canonical string form used in diagnostics and lowering.

Examples:

  • *int32
  • char8[]
  • (int32, float64)
  • func(int32, int32) -> int32

docs.title: Assignability rules
docs.text: The semantic analyzer uses a lenient compatibility model for unknowns and a narrower model for concrete numeric conversions.

docs.title: Backend layout
docs.text:
The lowering pipeline uses the type system to decide:

  • stack allocation size
  • field offsets
  • function argument passing
  • slice and interface representation
  • interface vtables
  • cast insertion

docs.title: Unions
docs.text:
Unions are user-defined composite types where multiple field names share the same storage.

A union gives one block of memory several possible shapes. At any moment, that memory should be interpreted as exactly one of the declared fields.

That makes unions useful for compact representations, low-level interop, tagged data models, and internal compiler or runtime structures.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
union Value {
    i: int32;
    f: float32;
    ptr: *opaque;
}

pub union TokenData {
    kind: uint32;
    payload: uint64;
}

A union declaration may include:

  • an optional pub modifier
  • a name
  • a field list
  • one type annotation per field

Union fields are written with semicolons.

docs.title: What a Union Means
docs.text:
A union is not a struct with extra memory tricks.

A struct stores each field separately. A union shares one storage area across all fields.

That difference matters a lot:

  • structs are for records where every field exists at once
  • unions are for alternatives where only one interpretation is active at a time

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Use a union when the alternatives are mutually exclusive.

union Number {
    i: int32;
    f: float32;
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not use a union just because it looks compact if the data is actually a record.

// bad idea: these values are not alternatives
union PersonLike {
    name: char8[];
    age: uint32;
    height: float32;
}

If all fields are supposed to be valid together, a struct is the correct choice.

docs.title: Union Declaration Syntax
docs.text:
The general shape of a union is:

union Name {
    field1: Type;
    field2: Type;
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
union Value {
    integer: int32;
    floating: float32;
    pointer: *opaque;
}

docs.title: Public unions
docs.text:
A union may be marked pub.

pub union ExportedValue {
    a: uint64;
    b: uint64;
}

Public visibility controls whether the union is visible outside the defining scope, following the same visibility rules used by the rest of the language.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The parser recognizes union declarations as top-level items.
  • Union declarations may appear at module level or inside nested modules.
  • The current parser does not show a type-parameter list for unions.

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
Each union field is declared as:

name: Type;

Example:

union FlagValue {
    bits: uint32;
    raw: uint32;
}

docs.title: Field rules
docs.text:
• Field names must be identifiers.
  • Each field must have a type.
  • Each field ends with a semicolon.
  • Field order is preserved in the declaration.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Keep field names descriptive of the active interpretation.

union PacketData {
    headerBytes: uint8[];
    checksum: uint32;
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not use vague field names like x, y, z unless the meaning is actually obvious.

union BadNaming {
    x: int32;
    y: float32;
    z: *opaque;
}

A reader should be able to tell what each alternative means without decoding the whole codebase.

docs.title: Field Type Rules
docs.text:
Fields may use any valid Rux type.

union Storage {
    byte: uint8;
    text: char8[];
    ptr: *opaque;
}

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Prefer small, explicit types when the goal is a compact representation.

union SmallValue {
    flag: bool8;
    code: uint8;
}

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not hide huge or unrelated payloads behind a union unless that is really the intended model.

union EverythingBagel {
    number: uint64;
    name: char8[];
    nested: SomeHugeStruct;
}

That kind of design can become unclear fast if the active alternative is not tracked carefully.

docs.title: Semantic Behavior
docs.text:
The compiler currently treats a union as a named type during semantic analysis.

The semantic analyzer records the union name in the type table and validates the field list.

From the reviewed implementation, unions are checked for:

  • duplicate field names
  • valid field type resolution

docs.title: Example diagnostic cases
docs.text:
union Bad {
    value: int32;
    value: float32;
}

union Bad {
    value: UnknownType;
}

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Keep union field sets small and obvious.

union ParseResult {
    ok: uint32;
    error: int32;
}

That kind of declaration is easy to reason about in both the compiler and the reader's head.

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not rely on a union to document meaning by itself.

A union only says the storage is shared. It does not automatically tell the reader which field is active.

If the program needs to know which field is currently valid, that state should usually be stored separately.

docs.title: Front End and Backend Status
docs.text:
Unions are currently supported in the front end.

The reviewed pipeline shows:

  • parser support for union Name { ... }
  • semantic-analysis support for registering unions as types
  • lowering metadata that carries union declarations forward

The reviewed files do not show a dedicated union-specific backend layout or code-generation path.

That means unions should be documented as a parsed and analyzed feature, not as a fully proven backend feature.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
Describe the current status honestly.

union Data {
    ptr: *opaque;
    id: uint64;
}

The syntax is real, but the backend story should be treated as implementation-dependent until the union path is explicitly finished.

docs.title: Bad practice
docs.text:
Do not claim full runtime support if the lower layers have not been verified yet.

That leads to docs that look polished but become misleading the moment someone tries to use the feature seriously.

docs.title: Simple union
docs.text:
union Number {
    integer: int32;
    floating: float32;
}

docs.title: Union with pointers
docs.text:
union Data {
    ptr: *opaque;
    id: uint64;
}

docs.title: Public union
docs.text:
pub union Payload {
    bytes: uint8[];
    count: uint32;
}

docs.title: Compact enum-like storage
docs.text:
union TokenData {
    kind: uint32;
    payload: uint64;
}

This is a reasonable union shape when the code needs one of several interpretations but does not want a larger record type.

docs.title: Missing field type
docs.text:
union Bad {
    value;
}

docs.title: Missing semicolon after a field
docs.text:
union Bad {
    value: int32
}

docs.title: Duplicate field names
docs.text:
union Bad {
    value: int32;
    value: float32;
}

docs.title: Unknown field type
docs.text:
union Bad {
    value: UnknownType;
}

docs.title: Bad practice: treating a union like a struct
docs.text:
union BadModel {
    name: char8[];
    age: uint32;
}

If both values are always needed together, this should be a struct instead.

docs.title: Keep the active variant obvious
docs.text:
A union is only safe when the program can tell which field is currently meaningful.

If that information is not stored somewhere, the code becomes easy to misuse.

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
Variables bind a name to a value.

In Rux, variables are introduced with let or var:

  • let creates an immutable binding.
  • var creates a mutable binding.

Variables follow lexical scope rules, which means a binding is only visible inside the scope where it was declared and any nested scope that can legally access it.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
let x = 42;
var count = 0;

let name: char8[] = "Rux";
var total: int32 = 10;

A variable declaration may include:

  • a binding name or pattern
  • an optional type annotation
  • an initializer
  • mutability (let or var)

docs.title: Immutable variable
docs.text:
let value = 123;

Creates a binding that cannot be reassigned after initialization.

docs.title: Mutable variable
docs.text:
var value = 123;

Creates a binding that may be reassigned later.

docs.title: Explicitly typed variable
docs.text:
let port: uint16 = 8080;
var index: uint = 0;

The declared type constrains the initializer and later assignments.

docs.title: Pattern declaration
docs.text:
Rux also supports declarations that bind multiple values through a pattern.

let (x, y) = GetPoint();

Pattern declarations are useful when the initializer returns a structured value.

docs.title: let
docs.text:
let introduces an immutable binding.

let x = 5;

After initialization, x cannot be assigned a new value.

docs.title: var
docs.text:
var introduces a mutable binding.

var x = 5;
x = 6;

A mutable binding may be reassigned, provided the new value is compatible with the variable’s type.

docs.title: Mutability and type compatibility
docs.text:
Mutability only controls whether reassignment is allowed. It does not change the type rules.

var value: int32 = 10;
value = 20;

This is valid because the assigned value matches the declared type.

var value: int32 = 10;
value = "hello";

This is invalid because the assigned value is not compatible with int32.

docs.title: Type Inference
docs.text:
Rux infers variable types from the initializer when possible.

let x = 42;
let y = 3.14;
let z = true;

In these examples, the compiler infers the variable types from the initializer expressions.

Type inference is convenient for local values where the type is obvious from the right-hand side.

docs.title: When inference is available
docs.text:
Inference is available when the compiler can determine the initializer type.

let message = "hello";
let answer = 42;
var counter = 0;

docs.title: When inference is not enough
docs.text:
If the compiler cannot determine the type from context, an explicit annotation is required.

let value: int32 = ComputeValue();

This is the preferred form when the initializer alone is not enough to establish the type.

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Use inference when the type is obvious. Use an explicit annotation when the type matters for readability, ABI, overload resolution, or precision.

docs.title: Explicit Types
docs.text:
A variable may declare its type explicitly.

let count: uint32 = 10;
var total: int64 = 0;

The initializer must be compatible with the declared type.

docs.title: Why explicit types matter
docs.text:
Explicit types are useful when:

  • the value should have a specific width
  • the value crosses an FFI boundary
  • the compiler would otherwise infer a broader or narrower type
  • the code must communicate intent clearly

Example:

let fd: int = OpenFile(...);

Here the type is explicit because the value is part of a system-level interface.

docs.title: Initialization Rules
docs.text:
A variable declaration normally includes an initializer.

let x = 10;
var y = 20;

In many cases, the initializer is required because the compiler uses it to determine or validate the variable’s type.

docs.title: Uninitialized declarations
docs.text:
If a declaration does not have an initializer, it must still be meaningful to the compiler. In practice, this means the declaration needs enough information to establish its type.

var value: int32;

That form can be used when the language permits an explicitly typed declaration without immediate initialization.

If a declaration cannot be typed or made valid without an initializer, it is rejected.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Variables obey lexical scope.

A binding is visible from its declaration point onward within the current scope and nested scopes, but not outside the scope where it was introduced.

{
    let x = 10;
    Print(x);
}

Print(x);

The second Print(x) is invalid because x is out of scope.

docs.title: Shadowing
docs.text:
A name can be reused in a nested scope.

let x = 1;

{
    let x = 2;
    Print(x);
}

Print(x);

The inner x shadows the outer one inside the block.

Shadowing is useful when a value is transformed and the original binding is no longer needed.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text:
Only mutable variables can be reassigned.

var counter = 0;
counter = counter + 1;

Attempting to assign to an immutable binding is an error.

let counter = 0;
counter = 1;

docs.title: Compound assignment
docs.text:
Rux supports compound assignment operators for common update patterns.

var value = 10;
value += 5;
value -= 2;
value *= 3;

These forms are only valid when the left-hand side is a valid assignable location and the operation is defined for the operand types.

docs.title: Assignment compatibility
docs.text:
The assigned value must be compatible with the variable’s type.

var x: int32 = 5;
x = 10;

Valid.

var x: int32 = 5;
x = 3.14;

Invalid unless an explicit conversion is performed.

docs.title: Destructuring
docs.text:
Rux supports declarations that bind from structured values.

let (x, y) = GetPoint();

This is useful when a function returns multiple values or a tuple-like value.

Pattern declarations are especially useful for unpacking results without introducing temporary variables.

let (width, height) = GetSize();

docs.title: Notes on patterns
docs.text:
Pattern bindings should be read as declarations, not as general-purpose assignment syntax.

That means the structure of the pattern must match the structure of the initializer.

docs.title: Simple immutable variable
docs.text: let answer = 42;

docs.title: Simple mutable variable
docs.text:
var tries = 0;
tries += 1;

docs.title: Explicit type annotation
docs.text: let port: uint16 = 8080;

docs.title: String value
docs.text: let name: char8[] = "Alice";

docs.title: Shadowing in a nested scope
docs.text:
let value = 10;

{
    let value = 20;
    Print(value);
}

Print(value);

docs.title: Destructuring
docs.text: let (x, y) = GetPoint();

docs.title: Assigning to an immutable variable
docs.text:
let x = 1;
x = 2;

docs.title: Using a variable outside its scope
docs.text:
{
    let x = 1;
}

Print(x);

docs.title: Missing type where the compiler cannot infer one
docs.text: let value;

docs.title: Type mismatch on assignment
docs.text:
var count: int32 = 0;
count = "hello";

docs.title: Pattern mismatch
docs.text: let (x, y) = GetSingleValue();

docs.title: Immutability does not mean deep immutability
docs.text: let prevents reassignment of the binding itself. It does not automatically imply that every referenced object is frozen forever unless the type system or runtime rules say so.

docs.title: Types are fixed after declaration
docs.text: A variable does not change type just because a later assignment uses a different expression.

docs.title: Prefer explicit types at boundaries
docs.text: Use explicit types where the value crosses modules, packages, or FFI boundaries.

docs.title: Prefer inference for local clarity
docs.text: When the initializer makes the type obvious, inference keeps code compact.
